channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Will Pakistan’s Iran Gamble Pay Off?
item.description: Islamabad could leverage its success as a mediator to secure its own interests.

item.title: Can Rutte Sweet-Talk Trump Enough to Soothe NATO Tensions?
item.description: The NATO chief seeks to manage the U.S. president while appeasing other alliance members.

item.title: Strange new EV-makers keep appearing in China
item.description: If you can make a robo-vacuum, you can make a car

item.title: Will Congo’s President Push a Third Term?
item.description: As Kinshasa faces multiple crises, a bill risks plunging the country further into instability.

item.title: Silicon Valley has much to learn from the spreadsheet jockeys it despises
item.description: Even if financial markets disagree

item.title: Treasury Chief Bessent Defends Trump’s Iran Deal
item.description: Vice President J.D. Vance isn’t the only one being trotted out to sell an unpopular deal.

item.title: 2 State-owned Banks in Kyrgyzstan Drop 131 Clients Due to Sanctions Risks
item.description: While the companies haven’t been named, what’s clear is that Bishkek wants to be seen making an effort to address the EU’s sanctions circumvention concerns.

item.title: Global warming has made Europe’s heatwave 2-4°C worse
item.description: The continent is warming faster than any other

item.title: The Middle East’s Great Divergence Is Underway
item.description: With economic and security interests split between great powers, the region is beginning to consider a third path.

item.title: Electronics can now be printed onto living tissues
item.description: From cow femurs to replacement hips and even living leaves

item.title: What Is Iran’s Nuclear Status Quo?
item.description: Before diplomacy can move forward, U.S. negotiators face the daunting task of reestablishing a baseline.

item.title: Do high-tech “addons” increase the chance that IVF will work?
item.description: A big new review suggests most such procedures don’t help

item.title: Trump’s Un-American Capitalism
item.description: The current US administration's openly corrupt style of crony capitalism is the antithesis of the institutional foundation on which the US economy was built. The winners in the new oligarchic competition are not those who make the best products, but rather those who are best at flattering the mad king.

item.title: Can the Manchester Model Save Britain?
item.description: Following a decades-long effort to lay the foundations for economic dynamism, Greater Manchester has become the UK’s fastest-growing region. While the Manchester model cannot be precisely replicated at the national level, it does carry important lessons for outgoing Prime Minister Keir Starmer's likely successor.

item.title: Pax Silica and Indo-Pacific Partners
item.description: Insights from Akhil Ramesh.

item.title: Why the Iran Deal Might Endure
item.description: Both Washington and Tehran need it.

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: Syria’s honeymoon is over
item.description: An arrest has damaged the government’s image, writes Gareth Browne, our Middle East correspondent

item.title: The New Geography of the World Cup
item.description: Migration, colonial history, and elite academies have redefined today’s national teams.

item.title: Beyond a Viral Video, What Did Bangladesh’s PM Accomplish in Malaysia?
item.description: A Malaysian video attracted attention to the trip and its geopolitical significance.

item.title: China’s De-Dollarization Drive Has Hit a Wall
item.description: Beijing can create financial instruments, but it cannot decree demand.

item.title: China Reopens a Business Channel to Japan While Political Ties Remain Frozen
item.description: A Japanese trade group’s Beijing visit, led by an LDP MP, signals a possible thaw in China-Japan relations.

item.title: Washington’s Middle East Military Presence Is Uniquely Counterproductive
item.description: Rather than ensuring the free flow of energy, U.S. intervention has only interrupted it.

item.title: How the Iran War Disrupted ASEAN’s Energy Transition
item.description: The conflict did not derail the transition but exposed how vulnerable it remains to external disruption – and how quickly governments can fall back on fossil fuels.

item.title: Risk Sharing Crosses Central Asian Borders: EBRD Deal Links a Kyrgyz Bank and an Uzbek Gym Chain
item.description: A bank lending a business money is hardly cause for a headline. But this is no ordinary loan.

item.title: Anthropic’s Export Control Crackdown Leaves South Korea Caught in Washington’s AI Crossfire
item.description: The suspension of Anthropic’s most advanced AI model has exposed how Silicon Valley lobbying shapes U.S. export policy at the expense of allied economies.

item.title: The Future of BRICS After the Iran War
item.description: Carlos Frederico Pereira da Silva Gama and host Tushar Shetty discuss the priorities and divergences of India’s BRICS presidency ahead of the September leaders’ summit.

item.title: Is There a Way to Break the Deadlock in Japan-China Relations?
item.description: Is this the new normal?

item.title: Not Just the Military: Xi Jinping’s Other Senior Party Purge
item.description: Xi has ruthlessly gone after former Politburo Standing Committee member and one-time economic czar Wang Qishan.

item.title: How Trillionaires Are Really Made
item.description: Some may argue that the world’s first trillionaire is a story of technological triumph and a win for American capitalism. But markets have not bet on cutting-edge technology or a company too big to fail, but rather on the idea of a mega-monopolist with wealth and influence so vast that the US government will not allow him to fail.

item.title: Cities Show How Climate Action Makes Life Better
item.description: Cities have shown that a combination of adaptation measures and energy efficiency can save lives, improve well-being, reduce energy costs, and create conditions for sustainable growth. A new partnership seeks to help mayors and city leaders scale up these efforts through technical and financial assistance.

item.title: Why big AI labs are hiring so many philosophers
item.description: The technology presents all sorts of thorny problems—a philosopher’s favourite kind

item.title: The Myth of Global Chaos
item.description: Today’s seemingly nonstop international tumult is not as unintelligible as it seems. What pundits lazily describe as chaos is the culmination of developments that were long in the making and arrived at a time when the international system was no longer able to prevent or mitigate geopolitical shocks.

item.title: ASEAN Looks Toward a Post-WTO Era
item.description: Can the bloc maintain centrality amid managed trade?

item.title: A Rastafarian prisoner cannot sue guards who shaved his locks
item.description: The Supreme Court is curtailing plaintiffs’ rights to damages

item.title: Russia Makes Inroads in Southeast Asia
item.description: Energy is Putin’s strongest card in a fuel-hungry region.

item.title: New Prime Minister, Same Problem
item.description: Why the consequences of Brexit can no longer be avoided.

item.title: Can Kazakhstan and Iran Sustain Momentum on Transport Corridor Development?
item.description: The Iran-U.S. conflict has served to concentrate attention on the transport relationship between Kazakhstan and Iran, in development for years before the war began.

item.title: With the Military Ascendant, Is This the End for Imran Khan?
item.description: The rapid consolidation of power by Field Marshal Asim Munir has the PTI reconsidering its options – and the controversial “charter of democracy.”
